title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Human memory is not a perfect recording of the past, (0) but rather a dynamic process that reconstructs events each time we recall them. (9) Although we often trust our memories implicitly, they are surprisingly susceptible to suggestion and distortion over a long period. (10) Such is the complexity of the brain that it can blend real experiences with imagined details without us even realizing. (11) Provided that we pay close attention to an event, the chances of accurate encoding into long-term storage are greatly increased. (12) Not only does sleep help consolidate these memories, but it also clears out irrelevant information that we no longer need. (13) Regardless of how vividly we remember something, the actual facts may differ significantly from our mental narrative of the event. (14) Not until researchers began studying false memories did they understand how easily the human mind can be deceived. (15) Despite these flaws, our ability to remember is essential for learning from our mistakes and planning for the future. (16) Whether we will ever develop technologies to enhance our natural memory capacity is a question that fascinates both scientists and science fiction writers. This intricate system remains a cornerstone of our identity.

example.text: Human memory is not a perfect recording of the past, (0) but rather a dynamic process.
example.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce a contrasting idea.

questions.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce a contrast.
Determiner used for emphasis before a noun phrase.
Conjunction meaning 'if' or 'on the condition that'.
Part of the 'Not only... but also' structure.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of'.
Part of the 'Not until' structure triggering inversion ('did they understand').
Preposition used to introduce a contrast or concession.
Conjunction introducing a doubt or two options.
